title: Replicating carbonaceous vug in synthetic porous media
author: Khan, DiCarlo, et al.

steps_raw.title: Vug surface extraction
steps_raw.instruction: ImageJ’s built-in find connected region algorithm is then used to find the connected spaces in 3D. The algorithm color codes and isolates the individual connected spaces. The desired vug space is selected and visualized in the built-in 3D viewer, and the surface (STL) is exported for 3D–printing.

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: A Guelph dolomite sample (38 mm × 72 mm) is scanned at a resolution of 250 μm [5] and thickness of 3 mm in a medical CT scanner (Universal Systems HD-350E located at UT PGE). The images are processed to isolate a vug and surface extracted. MakerBot® Replicator 2 (located at UT PGE) is used to 3D–print the surface. Mold is created and cast in gypsum cement (Fig. 1). Core is sintered with the casted vug in place and treated with acid to remove the gypsum cement.Fig. 1Surface visualization (left), resultant 3D–print (middle) and vug cast (right).Fig. 1

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Core is scanned in the medical CT scanner and vug space is reconstructed using Fiji-ImageJ (Fig. 2). The vug visualizations are compared to validate the repeatability of the system.Fig. 2Vug space after acid flood.Fig. 2

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: The extremities are not captured well in the reconstruction, but this can be attributed to the limited resolution of the 3D–printed vug space.

materials: •Carbonate (vug) images•Fiji-ImageJ software•3D–printer•Plaster of Paris•Play-Doh®•1.0 mm soda lime glass bead•Graphite mold•Muffle furnace•2 M Hydrochloric acid
